title: Concurrency-hygiene pair: runlease package-var seams (createTempFn/renameFn) are one t.Parallel() away from cross-test races; phaseobserver has sleep-based test sync (50-150ms), a per-select-iteration channel allocation, and split-brain locking discipline
summary: (1) runlease.go:39-43 exposes mutable package-level seams; tests at runlease_test.go:195-254 mutate+restore them while sibling tests call real Write through the same globals — safe only while nobody adds t.Parallel(). (2) phaseobserver: tests use bare time.Sleep(50-150ms) as startup barriers (phaseobserver_test.go:75,286; coverage_test.go:49) — flake source under CI contention; production Run() allocates a fresh make(chan time.Time) on EVERY select iteration via an inline func (phaseobserver.go:303-309) where the nil-channel idiom is the correct form; and locking is inconsistent — processLine/emit take obs.mu while the poll loop reads the same fields unlocked (fine single-threaded today, a trap for any future goroutine).
root_cause: Test seams grafted onto package state instead of injected; observer written single-threaded but half-armored with a mutex, inviting wrong conclusions.
fix: (1) Move createTempFn/renameFn into an injected writeConfig/Writer struct (constructor-default = real funcs); tests inject per-instance. (2) Hoist a nil stopCh before the loop (var stopCh <-chan time.Time; if stopTimer != nil { stopCh = stopTimer.C }); pick ONE locking story — Run is single-goroutine: remove obs.mu from processLine/emit and document 'not safe for concurrent use' (simplest), and replace test sleeps with a Ready channel / injected clock barrier.
acceptance: RED: runlease seam-isolation test proving two Writers with different injected seams don't interfere (package-var version cannot pass this)
phaseobserver: testing.AllocsPerRun pins 0 allocs/iteration for the stop arm; all sleep barriers replaced by deterministic signals; suite stable under -race -count=5 -parallel=8
connects_to: observer-sink-close-race (adapters/observer sibling — separate item, do first)
